subject: ANIMAL HUSBANDRY (ALT B)
preamble: This syllabus has been designed to portray animal husbandry as a trade for livelihood with emphasis on the acquisition of knowledge and entrepreneurial skills in animal husbandry. Candidates will be expected to answer questions on all the topics set out in the syllabus.
objectives: Basic animal production practices such as feeding, housing, pest and disease control
Efficient and effective management of animal enterprise
Efficient processing, preservation, packaging, storage and marketing of animal products
Basic entrepreneurial skills in animal husbandry related vocations
Basic knowledge and skills in animal improvement and health

examination_scheme.summary: The examination is for candidates in Nigeria only. It consists of three papers: Paper 1 (Multiple Choice), Paper 2 (Essay), and Paper 3 (Practical or Test of Practical Work).

papers.title: Multiple Choice
papers.details: Forty multiple choice questions all of which should be answered.

papers.title: Essay
papers.details: Six essay questions drawn from the entire syllabus. Each question carries 20 marks. Candidates will be required to answer four questions.

papers.title: Practical / Test of Practical Work
papers.details: Practical paper for school candidates and a test of practical work paper for private candidates. Consists of four questions all of which should be answered.

topics.title: Importance of Farm Animals
details: Source of food (meat, milk, eggs, honey etc).
Raw materials e.g. hide and skin, bones, hooves, hair/fur, egg shells.
Source of manure (fertilizer, bio-gas, bio fuel, growing of maggots and earthworms).
Source of feed ingredients (blood meal, bone meal, meat and bone meal, snail shell, egg shell, feathers etc).
Animal power (animal traction, transportation).
Research (laboratory, field), drugs, vaccines, hormones etc.
Source of employment.
Sales of products and by-products.
Social functions e.g. payment of bride price, cultural displays (weddings).
For security e.g. ducks, bees, turkeys.
As pets e.g. rabbits, sheep, chickens.
Sports and games e.g. horse racing, chicken fighting.
Religious festivals e.g. turkeys, rams etc.
Source of foreign exchange through export of animal products and by-products.

topics.title: Classification of Farm Animals
details: Classification: Simple stomach (non-ruminant or monogastric) e.g. poultry, pig, rabbits, horses, donkeys, snails, bees, grasscutters; Complex stomach (polygastric or ruminants) e.g. cattle, sheep and goat.
Identification of ruminants and non-ruminants based on external features, type of stomach, and type of feed consumed.

topics.title: Internal Organs and Their Functions in Farm Animals
details: Identification of internal organs (liver, lungs, heart, kidney, spleen, pancreas, stomach, crop, caecum, gizzard, small intestine, large intestine, tongue etc) and their functions.

topics.title: Body Systems and Their Functions in Farm Animals
details: Digestive, respiratory, nervous, circulatory, skeletal, reproductive systems and understanding their functions.

topics.title: Reproduction in Farm Animals
details: Definition of terms used in livestock reproduction: ovulation, oestrus cycle, heat period, signs of heat, mating, gestation, parturition, lactation, colostrum, flushing, steaming up, dystocia, vaginal prolapse etc.
Reproduction in livestock (mammals): detection of heat, mating systems, pregnancy detection and signs of parturition.
Reproduction in poultry: knowledge of the process of egg formation in poultry.
Reproductive hormones and their functions: sources and roles of female hormones (oestrogen, progesterone, relaxin, oxytocin etc) and male hormones (testosterone/androgen).

Management of pregnant farm animals: regular and adequate feeding, body exercise, steaming up, separation from male animals, provision of clean and adequate water, administration of drugs, dipping to eliminate ecto-parasites, parturition etc.

topics.title: Livestock Management Systems
details: Intensive, semi-intensive and extensive systems including advantages and disadvantages of each.

topics.title: Management Practices of Livestock
details: Housing requirements and use of local materials for construction.

Management practices: feeding, sanitation, hygiene, castration, dehorning, deworming, vaccination, inoculation, culling, debeaking, smoking (in bees), docking, identification methods, isolation, weaning, care of young animals.
Simple record keeping including income and expenditure accounts.
Management practices from birth to maturity of a named large ruminant, small ruminant, poultry, pigs, grasscutter, bees and snails.

topics.title: Meaning and Classes of Animal Feeds
details: Meaning of animal nutrition.
Feed nutrients (carbohydrates, proteins, fats and oil, vitamins, minerals and water), functions, sources, deficiencies.
Classification into concentrates, roughages, supplements, feed additives etc.

topics.title: Animal Feeds and Feeding
details: Livestock rations: meaning and types (balanced, maintenance, production rations).
Malnutrition: meaning, causes, symptoms, and ways to check malnutrition.

topics.title: Formulation of Livestock Rations
details: Practical diet formulations (starter, grower, finisher diets).
Identify feed ingredients (blood meal, fish meal, cotton seed cake, oyster shell, groundnut cake, maize grains, salt, premix, glycine etc).
Factors to consider in feed formulation.

topics.title: Processing and Marketing of Animal Products
details: Processing techniques: pre-slaughtering, slaughtering, post-slaughtering activities, hygienic conditions, slaughtering techniques, value addition.
Marketing of animal products: common marketing channels and agents (producers, wholesalers, retailers, consumers etc), advantages and disadvantages.

topics.title: Pasture Management
details: Meaning, examples, and importance of pasture and forage crops.
Types and features of pasture and forage crops.
Basic terminologies in pasture management.

topics.title: Rangeland Improvement
details: Meaning and features of rangelands.
Methods of rangeland improvement: reseeding, rotational grazing, controlled stocking, deferred grazing, controlled burning, fertilizer application, pest control, disease control etc.
Importance of rangeland: provision of vegetation and grasses, exercise, provision of hay and silage.

topics.title: Animal Improvement
details: Meaning and terminologies used in animal improvement.
Aims/importance: high reproductive efficiency, potency, mothering ability, cool temperament, high libido, resistance to pests and diseases, tolerance to harsh environment.
Methods used: introduction, selection, breeding, and their merits and demerits.

topics.title: Artificial Insemination
details: Meaning and explanation of artificial insemination.
Identification of materials, methods, steps, and precautions.
Advantages of artificial insemination.

topics.title: Farm Animal Diseases and Pathogens
details: Concept of farm animal disease: meaning and causal agents (bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoa).
General symptoms: loss of appetite, weight loss, diarrhoea, high temperature, blood stained urine, discharges, ruffled feathers/fur, sudden death etc.
Diseases of farm animals: main diseases, causal agents, mode of transmission, symptoms, simple preventive measures.
Factors predisposing farm animals to diseases: poor nutrition, poor sanitation, overcrowding, low immunity etc.

topics.title: Livestock Parasites and Pests
details: Livestock parasites: ectoparasites and endoparasites, life cycles, economic importance, control and prevention.
Livestock pests: storage pests and field pests, prevention, control, and economic importance.

topics.title: Practical Requirements and Identification
details: Products and by-products of farm animals: meat, eggs, milk, honey, hides, skin, blood, hair, wool, feathers, horn, hooves, bones, snail shell, animal dung, and their uses.
Identification of farm animals: describe, draw, and label external parts.
Internal organs and their functions in farm animals: major internal structures in ruminants, poultry, and pigs; differences in structures and functions.
Tools and equipment used in management: housing, brooding, feeding, identification, debeaking, castration, handling, dehorning, incubation, milking, slaughtering tools, and pasture tools.
Feeds and feedstuffs: common feed ingredients, crop residues, agricultural by-products, non-conventional feeds and major nutrients.
Pasture and forage crops: common pasture and forage crops, hay and silage making.
Artificial insemination practicals: tools, equipment, and simple techniques of semen collection, preservation, and insemination.
Pests and parasites: storage pests, field pests, ectoparasites, endoparasites, life cycles, prevention, and control.

appendices.title: FACILITIES AND MAJOR EQUIPMENT REQUIREMENTS
details: Farm Structures: Poultry/Pig House/Battery Cages (1), Cattle/Goat/Sheep Pen (1), Rabbit/Grasscutter Hutch (1), Snailery/Bee Hive (1).
Live Animals: Poultry/Pig (10), Rabbits/Grasscutter (10), Cattle/Sheep/Goat (10), Snails/Bees (50/100).
Specimens: Tick, Lice, Liver fluke, Tapeworm, Roundworm, Flea, Tsetse fly, Pests.
Charts & Pictures (5 each): Digestive systems, Reproductive organs, Skeletal, Circulatory, Muscular, Endocrine systems, Ovulation/Oestrus/Heat calendars, Animal diseases.

Machines & Equipment: Burdizzo pliers/castrators (2), Elastrators (2), Debeakers (2), Candlers (2), Dehorning saw (4), Ear notching knife (2), Electro-ejaculator (2), Artificial Inseminators (2), Knapsack sprayers (2), Refrigerators (2), Clinical thermometer (2), TV/CD Player/Projector (2 each).
Drugs & Feed Samples: First aid kits (2), Syringes/Needles (10), Vaccines, Feed samples (Bone meal, Blood meal, Fish meal, Groundnut cake, Maize grains, Mineral salt lick, etc., 1/2 kg each).
